actions.thought: In heads-up play, Ten-Six offsuit is a hand that should be played from the button/small blind. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a common opening size to exert pressure and take initiative in the hand, allowing for a continuation bet on many flops or folding to significant resistance.

In Heads-Up play, T5s is a marginal hand. While suited hands have potential, I am Out of Position (OOP) and facing a 3bb raise (300) rather than a standard min-raise. The pot odds require 33% equity, and while T5s technically has this raw equity against a wide opening range, realized equity OOP is significantly lower due to playability issues (domination, difficulty extracting value). Against an unknown opponent in the first hand of the tournament, defending T5s against a 3x open is likely -EV or marginal at best. I choose to fold to preserve chips and wait for better spots or positional advantage.

I am on the Button (SB) with Js 4d, which is a statistically weak hand (J4 offsuit). In heads-up play, while I have position post-flop, J4o falls outside the standard opening range (typically top 70-80% of hands). At 100BB deep, there is no need to get involved with the bottom of my range this early in the tournament. Folding is the standard EV-maximizing play here to conserve chips for playable hands.

I am on the button (SB) with Ace-Trey offsuit. In heads-up play, an Ace is a strong starting hand. Raising is preferred over calling to exert pressure and take control of the pot early. A standard open raise to 300 (3x the BB) is appropriate here.

I hold 7s 5h in the Big Blind. The opponent on the Button (SB) has raised to 300 (3bb). This hand is unsuited, unconnected, and generally very weak (7-high). While heads-up ranges are wide, 75o is near the bottom of the distribution. The opponent's 3x sizing offers me worse pot odds (need 33% equity) compared to a min-raise, and playing a weak hand out of position is unprofitable. My equity against a standard opening range is borderline at best, and equity realization will be poor. Folding is the correct standard play.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with J2s. While being suited gives this hand some potential, J2 is traditionally a weak hand due to the poor kicker, making it vulnerable to domination if a Jack hits. In Heads-Up play, opening ranges are wide (typically 80%+), but J2s sits near the bottom of that range. Given that I have folded the previous three hands and have not yet established a read on the opponent's defensive tendencies, playing a marginal hand like this could lead to difficult post-flop situations or bleeding chips against a potentially aggressive opponent. Folding here allows me to wait for a hand with better playability and equity without sacrificing significant stack depth early in the match.

T3s is a weak hand but has some playability post-flop. Given that 'Pro' has folded to all my previous raises and hasn't played a hand yet, I will continue to apply pressure from the button with a standard 3x raise. This range-based play exploits their current passivity.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (to 300) from the Button. My hand is T6o. While T6o has decent raw equity against a wide heads-up opening range (around 36-40%), the pot odds are 2:1 (need 33%), which is barely enough. However, being out of position with a hand that has poor playability (unconnected, off-suit) makes it difficult to realize that equity. Against a 3x sizing, I should defend a tighter range than against a min-raise. T6o falls below the threshold for a profitable defense OOP against this sizing.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with A7o, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play (well above the median). I have folded every hand so far (5 consecutive folds), including two buttons. To avoid being exploited as overly tight and to stop bleeding blinds, I must contest this pot. Raising is superior to limping as it builds the pot while I have the positional advantage and uncaps my range. A minimum raise to 200 is sufficient to apply pressure and define the range.

I am in the Big Blind holding 6h 5s. Pro, in the Small Blind, has raised to 200. To call, I only need to commit 100 more chips into a pot that will be 400 total. This gives me 3:1 pot odds, requiring roughly 25% equity. Against a typical heads-up opening range from the button/SB, 65o has sufficient equity to warrant a defense of the blind. I will call to see a flop and play post-flop.

On this board of 7h-7d-Kh, I have 6-high with no draw. As the Big Blind acting first post-flop in a heads-up game, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading here with a hand that has almost no equity or blockers doesn't accomplish much. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet, unless the turn improves my hand significantly or presents a good bluffing opportunity.

I have flopped trips with the best kicker (As 7c on 7h 7d Kh). This is a monster hand in heads-up play, effectively the nuts. My opponent (Flash) has checked to me. I have been very tight recently (5 folds), so a check back here creates deception. It disguises the strength of my hand, making it look like I missed the flop or have a medium pocket pair pot-controlling. This line induces bluffs on the turn from his air and allows him to catch up to a second-best hand (like hitting a pair) that can pay me off later. While checking risks giving a free card to a flush draw, the likelihood of him holding specifically a flush draw is lower than him holding air or a weak made hand that would fold to a bet. Checking maximizes expected value by keeping his wide range in the hand.

The turn 3s is a blank and does not improve my 6-high hand. Since the flop went check-check, it is possible Pro has some showdown value or is also holding air. I will check again. If Pro checks back, I see a free river. If Pro bets, I will likely fold as I have no real equity or draws on this board.

I hold Trip Sevens with an Ace kicker (As7c) on a board of 7h 7d Kh 3s. After checking back the flop to disguise my hand strength and induce bluffs, my opponent has checked to me again on the turn. It is now time to start building the pot to extract value. Checking again risks missing value from hands like Kx, flush draws, or pocket pairs (88-QQ) that might call a bet. A delayed continuation bet of half the pot (200) is small enough to get called by weaker hands and draws, while still building the pot for a river bet. If I check now, I likely only get one street of value on the river.

On a board of 7h-7d-Kh-3s, I am holding 6-high with no draws and no showdown value. After checking the flop and the turn, my opponent has bet 200 into a 400 pot. While the pot odds are 3:1, my hand has virtually zero equity against even a bluffing range that includes higher air or weak pairs. There is no reason to continue or attempt a bluff in this spot given I have no blockers to the value range and no potential to improve on the river.

K8s is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. As the button, I have positional advantage for the rest of the hand. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and exert pressure on my opponent, maintaining the aggressive betting pattern seen in previous hands.

In Heads-Up play, QJo is a strong starting hand (top ~15-20%). The opponent (Flash) has raised to 3x (300), which they have done consistently in previous hands. While I will be out of position post-flop, QJo is far too strong to fold. I have the option to 3-bet for value, but flat calling is a lower variance line that keeps the opponent's range wide and avoids bloating the pot while out of position against a player showing aggression. I need to call 200 chips to match the 300 bet.

I am out of position on a Ten-high dry flop (Ts 7c 3h) with two overcards (Queen, Jack). Standard heads-up strategy dictates checking to the pre-flop aggressor to see if they continue. Leading out (donk betting) has little merit here as it folds out worse hands and isolates me against better hands like Top Pair. I will check and evaluate based on my opponent's action.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the range advantage on this dry board of 3-T-7 rainbow. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300, which is 50% of the pot, to maintain aggression and potentially end the hand now. My King-high with a backdoor flush draw has equity, but betting forces my opponent to have a piece of the board to continue, allowing me to take down the pot if they missed.

I am Heads-Up Out of Position with QJo on a T-7-3 rainbow board. The opponent c-betted 300 (half pot). I have two overcards (Queen and Jack) and some backdoor straight potential. Against a wide button range that c-bets frequently, my hand has sufficient equity (roughly 6 outs to top pair, plus surviving against pure bluffs) to continue. The pot odds are 3:1 (calling 300 to win 900), requiring ~25% equity, which I have against most of his range. Folding here would be too tight given the heads-up dynamic and the strength of my high cards. Raising turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily. Calling allows me to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

I am Out of Position on the Turn with Queen-high on a T-7-3-5 board. The 5c brings a backdoor flush draw but does not improve my hand directly. I floated the flop c-bet with overcards, aiming to realize equity or catch a bluff. Leading out (donking) here would likely only get action from better hands (pairs) and fold out worse hands (bluffs) that I am currently beating or chopping with. Standard strategy dictates checking to the pre-flop aggressor who has position. If the opponent checks back, I get a free look at the river to potentially hit a Queen or Jack. If they bet again, I will have to re-evaluate, but for now, checking is the correct play.

After my continuation bet on the flop was called, the turn brings a 5 of clubs, which doesn't significantly change the board texture. My King-high has some showdown value against the opponent's potential missed draws (like J9 or QJ). Checking back here allows me to control the pot size and see a free river card. If the opponent has a marginal hand like a 7 or a weak 10, they are unlikely to fold to a second barrel on this board. Checking back also keeps my range protected.

I hold Queen-high (Qd Js) on a board of 3h Ts 7c 5c 4d. The river completes a potential straight for any 6 and puts four cards to a straight on the board (3-4-5-7). My opponent raised pre-flop, c-betted the flop, but checked back the turn. This line often indicates Ace-high, a weak pair, or a draw that missed. While I likely lose to Ace-high at showdown, turning my hand into a bluff here is high-risk given the coordinated board; the opponent might call with any pair or even Ace-high suspecting a bluff, or they might have checked back a 6 on the turn. Checking is the disciplined play to minimize variance and loss, allowing me to fold to a bet or potentially win/chop if the opponent checks back with a worse hand (though unlikely).

On this board texture, my King-high has limited showdown value but might still beat missed straight draws like QJ or J9. I checked the turn to control the pot and see a river. Pro has checked to me again on the river. While a bet might force out very weak pairs, Pro's call on the flop suggests they have at least some piece of the board, likely a Ten, Seven, or a small pocket pair, which they are unlikely to fold now. Checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown without risking more chips.

In a Heads-Up match with deep stacks (90bb+), the Small Blind (Button) typically opens a wide range (top 70-80% of hands). However, 7s6c (76 offsuit) lies on the borderline of this opening range. While it has connectivity, it lacks high card value and suit equity. Given that it is early in the tournament (Hand 8/150) and the opponent 'Flash' has shown aggression (raising 3x on their buttons and defending BB previously), playing a marginal hand like 76o carries a risk of facing a 3-bet or navigating a difficult post-flop board with weak holdings. I have previously folded J4o and J2o on the button, establishing a tighter image. Folding here reduces variance and preserves the stack for more profitable spots.

I am on the button with Jc 8s, which is a standard opening hand in heads-up play. Following my previous pattern of raising to 300 from this position, I will open to 3x the big blind to put pressure on my opponent and maintain a consistent aggressive image.

I am facing a 3bb raise (300 chips) from the Button while holding Q5o in the Big Blind. The pot odds require me to call 200 to win 600, offering 33% odds. While Q5o has raw equity close to this against a wide heads-up range, the 3x sizing punishes weak offsuit holdings significantly more than a min-raise. Additionally, being out of position with a hand that has poor playability (unsuited, low kicker, likely dominated if I hit top pair) makes it difficult to realize that equity. Flash has been consistently opening to 300, and while I need to defend to prevent being run over, Q5o is near the bottom of the defendable range against this specific sizing. I will fold and wait for a hand with better playability or a blocker to 3-bet.

I hold Pocket Aces, the best starting hand. In Heads-Up play, the Button (me) acts first pre-flop but has position (acts last) post-flop. I previously raised to 200 with A7o in Hand #6. To disguise my hand strength and balance my range, I will use the same sizing. A min-raise to 200 builds the pot while keeping the opponent's range wide, encouraging a call or a 3-bet which I can then punish.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 5s and the opponent has min-raised to 200. I only need to call 100 more into a pot that will be 400, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While 65o is a weak hand, it has some connectivity and the price is too good to fold in a heads-up match. I will call to see the flop and play from there.

I have middle pair (fives) on an Ace-high board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, checking is the standard play. This allows me to see how the opponent reacts to the board. If the opponent bets, I will likely call a reasonable sized bet given my pair and potential to improve or reach showdown cheaply.

I hold Top Set (AcAdAh) on a board of 5d 2c Ac. I have the effective nuts. While the board has a flush draw (two clubs), my hand creates a massive 'blocker' effect: it is virtually impossible for the opponent to have an Ace (top pair). Therefore, the opponent's range is heavily weighted towards total air, low pairs (2x, 5x), or flush/straight draws. If I bet now, I likely fold out all their air, winning a small pot. By checking back, I accomplish three things: 1) I disguise the strength of my hand, consistent with Hand #6 where I checked back trips; 2) I allow the opponent to catch a pair on the turn or gain confidence to bluff; 3) I control the pot size in a way that encourages action on later streets. The risk of giving a free card to a flush draw is acceptable given my strength and redraw potential.

I have a pair of fives on a board with an Ace and a Ten. Pro checked back on the flop, suggesting they might not have a strong Ace. However, I still only have third pair and out-of-position play is tricky. Checking again is the most prudent move to control the pot size and see if Pro wants to represent a hand or check behind. If Pro checks, I'm happy to take my pair to the river.

I hold Top Set (AdAh) on a board of 5d 2c Ac Ts. I have the effective nuts. Previously, I checked back the flop to disguise my hand strength and induce action, reasoning that I block top pair heavily. The Turn brings a Ten and maintains the flush draw. While I checked the flop to trap, checking the turn as well risks giving a free card to flush draws and misses value from hands like Tx or gutshots that have picked up equity. I need to start building the pot. A delayed c-bet of 200 (half-pot) extracts value from draws and second-best hands while balancing my range (mimicking the line taken in Hand #6).

I have a pair of fives (middle pair) on an Ace-high board. After we both checked the flop, Pro's bet on the turn could represent a wide range, including a Ten, a delayed continuation bet, or a pure bluff. Given the pot odds of 4:1 (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 800), folding is too conservative. My pair has decent showdown value in a head-up pot where the pre-flop aggressor checked the flop. I will call to see the river and evaluate.

On a board of 5d-2c-Ac-Ts-5h, I have improved to three of a kind (fives). The action so far has been: Pro raised pre-flop, we both checked the Ace-high flop, and Pro bet 200 on the Ten turn. Pro's check on the flop suggests they likely don't have a strong Ace. By leading out (donk betting) on the river for 400 (half pot), I am looking to get value from a Ten or a weak Ace that might check back if I check to them. My hand is very strong in this heads-up scenario, and I want to maximize value before the showdown.

I hold Aces Full of Fives (AdAh on 5d 2c Ac Ts 5h), which is the second nuts, losing only to Quad Fives (55). The opponent's river lead of 400 into a pot of 800 indicates strength, likely Trips (5x), a Straight (34), or a lower Full House (22). Given the board texture and my under-represented hand strength (having checked the flop), it is highly probable the opponent will call a raise with a worse value hand. Calling leaves value on the table. I will raise to 1200 to extract maximum value from 5x and Straights.

I have improved to three of a kind on the river. My lead for 400 was intended to extract value from a Ten or a weak Ace. Pro's raise to 1200 is a very strong signal, potentially indicating a better five, a full house, or a straight. However, my kicker is the Six, and while I am losing to many other five-x combinations (5-7 through 5-A), folding trips in a heads-up match after the flop was checked through is difficult. The pot odds are 3:1 (calling 800 to win a total pot of 3200), which makes a call the most mathematically sound decision, as Pro could occasionally be overplaying a strong Ace or a Ten, or representing the five as a bluff.

I'm on the button with T9o, which is a very strong hand for heads-up play and well within my raising range. Looking at my previous history, I've consistently raised to 300 from the small blind when entering a pot. Continuing this pattern maintains my table image while building a pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability. Pro has shown a mix of folding and defending, so a standard 3x raise is the most balanced and effective move here.

I hold A7s in the Big Blind, which is a strong hand in heads-up play, well above the median hand strength. The opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3bb) from the button, consistent with their pattern of opening frequently and aggressively (3x) in previous hands. Against a wide button opening range, A7s has excellent equity and dominates many of their speculative hands. While a 3-bet is a valid option to punish a wide range, calling is the standard and lower-variance play to defend the blind, keeping the opponent's range wide and avoiding bloating the pot out of position with a medium-strength Ace. I will call the additional 200 chips.

I am out of position in the Big Blind with Ace-high and a backdoor flush draw on a Jc-Tc-3s board. The opponent (Flash) was the pre-flop aggressor. Standard strategy dictates checking to the raiser to see their action. The board texture interacts well with many broadway hands in the opponent's range, so leading out (donk betting) is not advisable. I will check and evaluate based on whether they bet and the sizing of their bet.

I have middle pair (Tens) on a relatively wet board with a jack and two clubs. Pro checked to me, which is a standard play for the big blind defender. I want to bet here for both value and protection; value from draws (like straight draws or flush draws) and protection against overcards that could peel a card for free. A bet of 300 (half pot) is standard for this texture to define the strength of my hand while keeping the pot manageable if I'm already beat by a Jack or a slow-played set.

I hold As7s on a Jc-Tc-3s board. I checked and the opponent bet 300 (half pot) into a pot of 600. While I have Ace-high and a backdoor spade flush draw, this board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which contains many broadway combinations (KJ, QJ, AT, KT, QT, etc.) and draws. Calling out of position with just Ace-high and weak backdoor equity is a losing play in the long run against a standard c-bet range on this texture. I am likely behind or flipping at best, and turn playability will be difficult. Preserving my stack is the prudent decision.

I hold 7d 2s, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em. Despite having the Button, my opponent has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind against raises. Playing this hand has negative expected value compared to folding, which costs nothing extra. Therefore, the disciplined and correct play is to fold immediately.

I am on the button with 4-5 offsuit. While I have been opening for 300 (3x) on most of my buttons and seeing some success with folds from Pro, 4-5 offsuit is near the bottom of the starting hand range. Given my current 3x sizing, I should be slightly more selective than if I were using a 2x or 2.5x sizing. Pro has shown a willingness to defend the big blind in Hand 7 and Hand 11. I will choose to fold this hand to maintain a more solid range and avoid playing a potentially difficult pot out of position post-flop with such a weak hand.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 8c 4d, which is an extremely weak offsuit hand (bottom 20% range). In heads-up play, while ranges should be wider, this hand has very poor playability and equity. Folding is the standard play here to conserve chips.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Historically, I have been raising to 300 from the small blind, and my opponent has folded several times to this sizing. Given the strength of my hand, I will continue with this aggressive approach to build the pot or take it down immediately.

I hold a premium hand (King of Hearts, Ace of Diamonds) in the Big Blind. The opponent, Flash, has consistently opened for 3x (300 chips) from the button in previous hands. AKo is a top-tier heads-up hand that plays well for value. While calling is an option to keep the pot small out of position and keep the opponent's range wide, a 3-bet is superior here to extract value from worse Ax/Kx hands, charge draws, and punish the large open size. A 3-bet size of roughly 3.5x-4x the open is standard out of position. Raising to 1100 puts pressure on the opponent and builds a pot with a strong equity advantage.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand heads-up, especially in position. While my opponent has shown aggression with a 3-bet, folding here would be too tight given the range Pro could be 3-betting with. I will call to see a flop and utilize my positional advantage.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot with AKo on a Js-Jd-Tc-5s board (J-T-5 rainbow). This board texture interacts heavily with the opponent's pre-flop calling range (Pocket pairs, suited connectors, broadways like KJs, QJs, JTs). While I have range advantage with overpairs (AA, KK, QQ), my specific hand is currently Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw. Betting here folds out worse hands (low pairs, weak Ax) but isolates me against better hands (pairs of J or T) or strong draws. Additionally, the opponent has shown aggression on similar textures (raising flop in Hand 11 on J-T-3). Checking allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity (overcards + gutshot), and potentially check-call a reasonable bet or see a free turn.

I have middle pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a J-T-5 board. My opponent 3-bet pre-flop and has now checked. While my hand is relatively strong in heads-up play, checking back here allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and avoid a potential check-raise from a stronger hand like AJ or JJ. I can evaluate the turn and potentially catch bluffs or extract value later.

On the turn, the board becomes 5s Jd Tc 9d. This card heavily favors the opponent's range, as they are more likely to have calling hands like QJ, T9, J9, 88, or Qx that connect with the J-T-9 texture. Although I have improved to the Nut Flush Draw (with the Ad) and still hold a gutshot to the Broadway straight (needing a Queen), my hand is currently just Ace-high. Betting into a board that favors the opponent's range is risky and could lead to being raised off my equity or isolating myself against better hands. Checking allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity for free if the opponent checks back, or evaluate a bet if they decide to fire. Given the wet board texture and my out-of-position status, a check is the prudent play to navigate this high-equity spot without over-committing.

I have middle pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a board that is becoming quite coordinated (5s-Jd-Tc-9d). Pro 3-bet pre-flop but has now checked both the flop and the turn. This suggests they might have a hand like AK/AQ that missed or they are pot-controlling a marginal hand. By checking back, I control the pot size, realize my equity, and avoid a potential check-raise on a dangerous board. My hand has significant showdown value, and I'm happy to take a free river card to see if I can improve or reach a cheap showdown.

I hold the nut straight (Broadway) on a board of 5s Jd Tc 9d Qs with [Kh Ad]. The board features a 4-card straight (9-T-J-Q), meaning any King splits the pot and any 8 makes the low straight. Crucially, holding a King blocks the opponent's ability to have the nut straight, increasing the likelihood that I scoop the pot rather than chop. The opponent's passive line (checking back flop and turn) suggests a marginal made hand (like a set, two pair, or weak jack) or a missed draw. It is unlikely the opponent will bet these hands if I check, given the coordinated board. To extract value from an 8 (lower straight) or a stubborn two-pair/set, I should lead out. A bet of roughly half the pot targets these worse hands without forcing them to fold immediately.

On a board of 5s-Jd-Tc-9d-Qs, the runout is extremely coordinated. My pocket pair of Tens has become a very weak hand (effectively fourth pair) as any King or Eight makes a straight. Pro 3-bet pre-flop, which places a high frequency of Kings (AK, KK, KQ, KJs) and Queens (AQ, QQ) in their range. After checking the flop and turn, Pro's 1100 bet on the river—following the completion of multiple straight draws and the arrival of an overcard (the Queen)—looks like a value bet from a hand that either improved or was already beating my Tens. Given the board texture and the pre-flop action, I have very little showdown value here and calling is likely unprofitable.

In the Small Blind (Button) with A4s (Ace-Four of hearts), I have a strong heads-up starting hand. Suited Aces play very well in position as they can make nut flushes and often dominate weaker Aces or King-highs that call. My opponent has folded to some raises previously. Raising to 200 (min-raise) allows me to take the initiative, potentially take down the blinds immediately, or play a pot in position with a hand that has good equity and playability.

I have 8c 6c in the Big Blind. The opponent (Pro) raised to 200 from the small blind/button. I need to call 100 more into a total pot that will be 400. This gives me 3:1 pot odds, which is more than enough to justify calling with suited connectors. I will see a flop and evaluate my equity then.

I have middle pair (sixes) on a King-high board with a backdoor flush draw. In heads-up play, as the Big Blind, I am first to act post-flop. Since Pro was the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play here to see how they react to this board. My hand has decent showdown value and some potential to improve, so I want to keep the pot manageable.

I hold Ace-high (A4) on a K-6-2 board with a flush draw. While I have the range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor (representing the King), my specific hand has showdown value but is vulnerable. My opponent's range includes many Kx hands that defend the Big Blind, as well as flush draws. I do not have a club or a strong draw to semi-bluff with. Betting risks facing a check-raise or getting called only by better hands (pairs) and strong draws, while folding out hands I already beat. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, control the pot size, and potentially catch an Ace or Four on the turn, or evaluate based on the opponent's turn action.

The turn brings a second King, pairing the board. I have two pair (Kings and Sixes). On the flop, both of us checked. Pro's check on the flop suggests they likely don't have a King, as they would usually bet for protection or value. By checking the turn, I can induce a bluff from Pro if they have air, or get to a cheap showdown. My hand is strong enough to call a standard bet, but betting myself might fold out hands I can beat or get me into trouble if Pro is trapping with a King or a pocket pair like 77-QQ.

The board is now [2c, Kc, 6s, Ks]. The turn card paired the top card of the board, reducing the likelihood that the opponent holds a King, but also making it harder for me to represent one given my check on the flop. I hold Ace-high, which currently beats all of the opponent's missed draws (clubs and spades) and pure air. Betting would likely only fold out hands I am already beating (like Q-high) or get called by hands that beat me (pairs of 6s, 2s, or pocket pairs). By checking, I control the pot size, realize my equity for free, and keep the opponent's range wide, allowing me to potentially win at showdown with Ace-high or re-evaluate on the river.

On the river, the board is 2c Kc 6s Ks 3h. I have two pair, Kings and Sixes. The action has been checked through on the flop and the turn. Pro's checks suggest they likely do not have a King or a strong pocket pair. My hand has significant showdown value in this small pot. By checking, I allow Pro to bluff with air or simply take the hand to a free showdown where I am likely ahead of their range (which consists of missed draws, Ace-high, or small pocket pairs). Betting might only get called by hands that beat me or marginal hands that might have folded anyway, so checking is the most prudent way to realize my equity.

I hold A4o on a board of 2c Kc 6s Ks 3h. My final hand is a Pair of Kings with an Ace kicker (K-K-A-6-3). The opponent has checked to me on the flop, turn, and now river. This line typically indicates either a weak made hand (like a small pair of 2s, 3s, or 6s) attempting to get to showdown cheaply, or a missed draw (flush draws from flop or turn). If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands (like Queen-high missed draws), and I risk getting called only by better hands (any pair or better Kicker). While a bluff might fold out a small pair, the pot is small (400 chips), and I have sufficient showdown value to beat all missed draws and pure bluffs. Checking back maximizes my Expected Value by realizing my equity against the bottom of their range without risking chips against the top.

I am on the button with T7o. In heads-up play, T7o is a reasonable hand to open for a raise to apply pressure on the big blind. I have been consistently raising to 300 from the button in previous hands to maintain an aggressive image.

I hold Pocket Queens (Qh Qc) in the Big Blind, a premium starting hand. The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 from the Button, consistent with his frequent 3x opening strategy. Holding Ten-Eight suited on the button is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. I will continue my strategy of opening for 3x the big blind to 300. This hand has great playability post-flop and allows me to maintain aggression from position.

I am Out of Position in a 3-bet pot with A-high on a paired K-K-9 board. The board texture is very dry and static. It allows me to realize the equity of my backdoor diamond draw and backdoor straight possibilities without risking a check-raise. If Pro is trapping with a King, I avoid losing more chips now; if Pro has a hand like Ace-high, I get to see the turn for free.

I am Out of Position in a 3-bet pot with Ace-high on a board of Kd-Ks-9h-4h. The turn card is a relative blank, though it brings a second heart. By checking, I keep his bluffing range in the hand and attempt to get to showdown cheaply with my showdown value. If he checks back again, I have a good chance to win or evaluate on the river. This follows the passive line taken on the flop to control the pot size.

The turn 4h is a blank that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Pro has checked for a second time after 3-betting pre-flop. I hold AdJd (Ace-high) on a Kd-Ks-9h-4h-8h board. The pot is 2200. After 3-betting pre-flop and checking down the flop and turn, my hand has showdown value but is likely too thin to bet for value. On the river 8h, I have improved to a pair of Eights. The board is Kd-Ks-9h-4h-8h. Pro has checked three streets in a row after 3-betting pre-flop. I hold Ace-Queen suited (Ad Qd) on the Button, which is a premium hand in Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em. My strategy is to raise for value to build the pot while having position post-flop. I have flopped Trip Queens with the best kicker (Ace), a very strong hand. The board contains a flush draw (two spades). I hold Trip Queens with an Ace kicker, a very strong hand. I checked back the flop to disguise my strength. I have an open-ended straight draw with 8s and 6c on a board of Qs-Qh-9s-Th. I need a 7 or a Jack to complete the straight. The river board of Qs-Qh-9s-Th-Tc is double-paired. My hand (8s-6c) does not improve upon the board's two-pair (Queens and Tens with a Nine kicker). Effectively, I am 'playing the board'. Given that Pro bet on the turn, it is highly likely they hold at least one of these cards. I hold the virtual nuts with Queens Full of Tens (Qs Qh Ad Th Tc -> QQQTT). I will bet approximately 70% of the pot to extract maximum value from a Ten, while being unconcerned about a check-raise as I am effectively uncapped.

The board is Qs-Qh-9s-Th-Tc, creating a double-paired board of Queens and Tens with a Nine kicker. My hole cards (8s-6c) do not improve this hand, meaning I am 'playing the board'. I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a weak kicker (7) on a board of A-8-4-6-3. I have Queen-high on an Ace-high board with very little showdown value. The board texture is 4s-Ac-8d-6h-3s, which is extremely dry. The turn brings the Queen of Spades (Qs), making the board Ks-6d-Tc-Qs. This is an exceptionally bad card for my hand (Pocket Sevens). The river is the Nine of Hearts (9h), making the final board Ks-6d-Tc-Qs-9h. This is a very coordinated board where any Jack makes a straight (K-Q-T-9-J). I plan to check and evaluate their action, likely calling or check-raising if they bet.

By calling, I keep the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue bluffing on future streets or value-own themselves with worse Tens. Calling is the most prudent play to control the pot size out of position while extracting value from aggressive bluffs.

By checking, I keep the opponent's range wide, allowing them to bluff with a lone club or a weaker hand, or check back for pot control. If they bet, I have a very easy call given my pair and strong draw. Checking maximizes my ability to realize equity and extract value from aggressive plays.

KJo is a very strong hand in heads-up play. I will open with my standard 3x raise to 300 from the button to build the pot and take control of the hand in position.

I am on the Button (SB) with J7o. In heads-up play, this hand is strong enough to play, generally falling within a standard opening range (top ~55% of hands). I am in the big blind with 9s 4c, a relatively weak hand. The opponent, Pro, has raised to 200, which is a standard min-raise from the button. I am on the button with 8d 4d. This is a suited connector/gapper that plays reasonably well post-flop in a heads-up situation. I am in the Big Blind with Jd9d facing a standard 3x open raise from the Button (Flash). I hold Jd9d on a Qd Qh Tc board. I have an open-ended straight draw (need a King or an 8) and a backdoor flush draw. Being out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. My hand is strong enough to call a continuation bet, but not strong enough to lead or check-raise for value immediately without improving. On a paired board of Qd-Qh-Tc, I am the pre-flop aggressor. Pro has checked to me. While my 8d 4d has not connected with the board yet, this is a good texture for a continuation bet. I hold Jd9d on a Qd-Qh-Tc board, giving me an open-ended straight draw (outs: any King or 8) and a backdoor flush draw. The opponent bets 300 into a pot of 600 (half-pot), offering me 3:1 pot odds. With 8 clean straight outs, I have about 17% equity to hit on the turn and roughly 32% by the river, which satisfies the direct pot odds requirement, plus I have implied odds if I complete my hand against a Queen or overpair. Raising on this paired texture is risky as the opponent's range connects well with Queens and Tens, making folds unlikely. Calling is the standard and most profitable play to realize my equity.

I am out of position on the Turn with an open-ended straight draw (Jd9d on Qd-Qh-Tc-Ah). The Ace on the turn heavily favors the opponent's pre-flop raising and flop c-betting range (Ax, Qx, broadways). If they check back, I take a free card to try and hit my straight (Kings or Eights). If they bet, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing and pot odds.

The board has developed into Qd-Qh-Tc-Ah. As the pre-flop aggressor who also continuation bet the flop, the Ace on the turn is an ideal card for my range. Pro called the flop bet, which typically indicates a Ten, a mid-pocket pair, or a straight draw. However, the Ace is a significant scare card for those hands. Pro's check on the turn allows me to represent a strong hand like an Ace or better. I am facing a half-pot bet (600 into 1200) on the turn with the board reading Qd-Qh-Tc-Ah. I hold Jd-9d, giving me an open-ended straight draw (outs are Kings and Eights). I need to call 600 to win a total pot of 2400, requiring 25% equity. With 8 outs and one card to come, my raw equity is approximately 17.4% (8/46). This is insufficient to call based on direct odds. Furthermore, the board is paired (Queens), which introduces significant reverse implied odds; if I make my straight, I could still lose to a Full House (which is well within the opponent's range given the action and board texture involving broadway cards). The Ace on the turn heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Given the poor direct odds and the dangerous board texture, folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack.

I hold A2o on the Button (SB). In heads-up play, any Ace has good equity and is a standard opening hand. In a heads-up scenario, Queen-Trey suited (Qs 3s) is a hand that is well within the defending range against a standard 2x button raise. On a board of 7d 4s Tc, I have Queen-high with a backdoor spade flush draw and two overcards to the lower board cards. I hold Ace-Deuce offsuit on a Ten-Seven-Four rainbow board. After raising pre-flop and getting called, I have missed the flop but retain Ace-high showdown value. On the Turn Jc, the board is now 7d 4s Tc Jc. Since the opponent checked back on the flop (7d 4s Tc), it's less likely they have a strong hand like a Ten or a set. I hold Ace-Deuce offsuit on a board of 7-4-T-J. After checking back the flop, the turn Jack connects with many hands in the Big Blind's defending range (e.g., Jx, QJ, KJ, T9, straight draws). On the river, the board has paired sevens (7d 4s Tc Jc 7h). My hand is Queen-high, which has almost no showdown value against Pro's range, even after they checked back on both the flop and the turn. Their checks suggest a weak made hand like Ace-high, King-high, or a small pocket pair, all of which currently beat me. I will check and hope for a rare check-back from a worse hand (like 9-8 or 6-5), but I am prepared to fold if Pro bets, as my hand lacks the strength to call any significant action.

The board is 7d 4s Tc Jc 7h. I hold As 2d. My final hand is a pair of Sevens with an Ace kicker (7-7-A-J-T). My opponent has checked to me on all three streets (flop, turn, river). Better hands (any pair like 22+, 4x, Tx, Jx) will likely call, so I lose more chips. There is little merit in turning my hand into a bluff here. Checking back realizes my showdown equity and wins against his missed range.

The board is 2h-Qc-5s-4h-Tc. I have Jack-high with no showdown value against most of Pro's pre-flop raising range (which includes many Ace-high and King-high hands). I am in the Big Blind with pocket fours. Pre-flop, I 3-bet and followed up with a continuation bet on an Ace-high flop, which Pro called. The turn is a Jack of Hearts. On the Turn, the board is [3s, Ah, 8d, Jh]. I hold [8h, 6h], giving me a pair of Eights and a heart flush draw. My opponent, who 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet the flop, has checked to me. On the river, the board is 3s Ah 8d Jh 6d. I 3-bet pre-flop and continuation-bet the Ace-high flop, which Pro called. On the river, I have improved to two pair (Eights and Sixes). A bet of 1000 (half pot) is large enough to get value but small enough to get called by top pair.

On the river, the board is 3s-Ah-8d-Jh-6d. I 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet the flop, representing strength. Pro called the flop bet and then checked back the turn. I hold the Jack of Hearts on a monotone Ah 9h 5h board. This gives me a strong flush draw (3rd nut flush draw). My opponent has bet half-pot (300 into 600). The turn is the 9d, making the board 9h 5h Ah 9d. This pairs the board, introducing full house possibilities, though my primary equity comes from the Jack-high flush draw (Jh). I am out of position. I have top pair with an Ace, but the board texture is becoming increasingly dangerous. The nine paired on the turn, and there are still three hearts on the board from the flop. On the River, the board is [9h 5h Ah 9d 4d]. I hold [Jh Td]. My hand is a pair of Nines with an Ace-Jack kicker (playing the board 9-9-A-5-4, using J as the second kicker over the 5). Flash has top pair of Aces with an 8 kicker. The board is quite dangerous with three hearts and a paired nine. Pro has checked three times (Flop, Turn, River). I hold King-high (Kc 6d) on a flop of Ace-Seven-Jack with two spades.
